wonder: Why does sleep feel instant after falling asleep, even though falling asleep can feel slow?
activity_summary: Learner discussed the difference between the process of falling asleep and the experience of being asleep. The discussion connected subjective time, consciousness, rest, and waking up in the morning.
learner_explanation: Sleep feels instant after you fall asleep, but getting there can feel slow. If you had to feel every second of the night, it would feel horrible.

parent_review.review_note: Accepted as evidence of reflective reasoning about time perception, consciousness, and lived experience.

subject_mappings.subject: science
subject_mappings.evidence_note: Learner discussed sleep, waking, and subjective experience.

subject_mappings.subject: language arts
subject_mappings.evidence_note: Learner expressed a multi-part idea and used comparison to explain it.

subject_mappings.subject: philosophy
subject_mappings.evidence_note: Learner considered whether conscious experience changes the meaning of time.

evidence_items.description: Learner described sleep as instant after falling asleep and imagined the opposite case as unbearable.

next_questions: What happens to our sense of time during dreams?
Why does waiting feel slow but sleeping feels fast?
How could we keep a sleep journal without making sleep harder?
non_capture_note: This receipt records one learning event and should not be used as a fixed identity label.
